section.title: Justified by Faith, Not Works
section.sectionRationale: Here the letter reaches its theological heart. Paul recounts confronting Peter at Antioch for withdrawing from Gentile believers, and turns the incident into the clearest statement of the gospel: a person is not justified by works of the law but by faith in Jesus Christ. Paul has died to the law and been crucified with Christ, and he refuses to nullify the grace of God, for if righteousness came through the law, then Christ died for nothing.
section.thesis: No one is justified by works of the law but only by faith in Jesus Christ; the believer has been crucified with Christ so that Christ now lives in him, and to seek righteousness through the law is to set aside the grace of God and make Christ's death pointless.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the confrontation at Antioch when Peter drew back from Gentiles; the great thesis that justification is by faith in Christ, not works of the law; and the crucified life, I have been crucified with Christ, and the refusal to nullify grace.

units.label: Confronting Peter at Antioch
units.note: not walking in line with the truth of the gospel

verses.text: When Cephas came to Antioch, however, I opposed him to his face, because he stood condemned.
For before certain men came from James, he used to eat with the Gentiles. But when they arrived, he began to draw back and separate himself, for fear of those in the circumcision group.
The other Jews joined him in his hypocrisy, so that by their hypocrisy even Barnabas was led astray.

When I saw that they were not walking in line with the truth of the gospel, I said to Cephas in front of them all, “If you, who are a Jew, live like a Gentile and not like a Jew, how can you compel the Gentiles to live like Jews?”

units.label: Not by works of the law but by faith
units.note: the great thesis of the letter

verses.text: We who are Jews by birth and not Gentile “sinners”

know that a man is not {{dikaioo|justified}} by works of the {{nomos|law}}, but by {{pistis|faith}} in Jesus Christ. So we, too, have believed in Christ Jesus, that we may be justified by faith in Christ and not by works of the law, because by works of the law no one will be justified.

units.label: Crucified with Christ
units.note: I no longer live, but Christ lives in me

verses.text: But if, while we seek to be justified in Christ, we ourselves are found to be sinners, does that make Christ a minister of sin? Certainly not!
If I rebuild what I have already torn down, I prove myself to be a lawbreaker.
For through the law I died to the law so that I might live to God.
I have been {{systauroo|crucified with Christ}}, and I no longer live, but Christ lives in me. The life I live in the body, I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave Himself up for me.
I do not set aside the grace of God. For if righteousness comes through the law, then Christ died for nothing.

dikaioo.gloss: To justify, to declare righteous, to acquit. This is a courtroom word: God the judge pronounces the guilty sinner righteous in His sight. Paul insists this verdict comes not by works of the law but by faith in Christ. Justification is not being made gradually better; it is being declared right with God on the basis of Christ's finished work.

nomos.gloss: Law, especially the law of Moses with its commands. Works of the law are the deeds the law requires, including its badges of covenant membership like circumcision. Paul's point is that no amount of law keeping can justify a sinner, because the law can command righteousness but cannot supply it. The law was never given to be the way of acceptance with God.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance. Justifying faith is not a work we perform to earn favor but the empty hand that receives what Christ has done. Paul contrasts faith in Jesus Christ with works of the law as two utterly different grounds of acceptance. Faith looks away from self to the Son of God who loved me and gave Himself for me.

systauroo.gloss: To crucify together with. Paul says he has been crucified with Christ: in union with Him, his old self under the law's condemnation has died, and the risen Christ now lives in him. This is the deep logic of the gospel, that the believer is so joined to Christ that His death and life become the believer's own.

basic.context: Paul tells of the day he had to correct Peter himself. At Antioch Peter had freely eaten with Gentile believers, but when strict Jewish visitors arrived, he drew back and separated himself, and others followed, even Barnabas. Paul saw this as a betrayal of the gospel and confronted Peter publicly. Then he states the heart of the whole letter: no one is justified by works of the law, only by faith in Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: The Antioch story is not gossip; it sets up the doctrine. Peter's behavior implied that Gentiles were second class until they kept Jewish law, which denies that faith in Christ is enough. Paul's response gives us the classic definition of justification. Read verses 15 to 21 as the theological center of Galatians.
basic.originalLanguages: The key word is dikaioo, to justify, to be declared righteous by God the judge. It comes not by works of the nomos, the law, but by pistis, faith, in Jesus Christ. And Paul describes union with Christ by systauroo, crucified together with Him, so that Christ now lives in him.
basic.typology: The law that pronounced a curse on lawbreakers is answered by Christ who bore that curse. Dying to the law so as to live to God fulfills the pattern of death and resurrection: the old life under condemnation ends, and new life in Christ begins, which the sacrifices and the exodus only pictured.
basic.christ: Christ is everything here. He is the object of justifying faith, the one who loved me and gave Himself up for me, and the one whose death is emptied of meaning if righteousness could come by law. The believer is crucified with Him and now lives by Him. If we could be saved by works, then Christ died for nothing.
basic.god: God is the judge who justifies, declaring sinners righteous on the basis of Christ, not works. He is the God whose grace Paul refuses to set aside. The whole passage guards the freeness of God's grace: to add works is to insult the God who gave His Son so that we might be accepted for Christ's sake alone.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3 says all are justified freely by grace through faith in Christ. Romans 6 speaks of being united with Christ in His death and raised to new life. Ephesians 2 says we are saved by grace through faith, not by works.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible asks how a sinner can stand before a holy God. Here is the answer at its clearest: not by our works but by faith in Christ, who was crucified in our place. The believer, crucified with Christ, now lives a new life by faith in the Son of God who loved him.

context.literary: The confrontation narrative in verses 11 to 14 flows without a clear break into the doctrinal statement of verses 15 to 21, so that it is hard to say where Paul's words to Peter end and his words to the Galatians begin. That blurring is intentional: the point made to Peter is the point made to Galatia. Verse 20 rises to some of the most personal and moving language in all of Paul.
context.historical: Antioch was a mixed church of Jewish and Gentile believers who shared meals together. When men came from James, likely urging strict separation, Peter's withdrawal effectively told Gentile Christians they were not full members without adopting Jewish practice. Table fellowship was the flashpoint because it embodied whether the gospel truly united the two.
context.canonical: Galatians 2:16 is one of the fountainhead texts of the doctrine of justification by faith, echoed and expanded in Romans. Verse 20 became a treasured summary of union with Christ. The passage was central to the Reformation recovery of the gospel of grace.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that even an apostle can betray the gospel in practice, and to state with full clarity that justification comes by faith in Christ and not by works of the law, so that no one adds law keeping to grace without emptying Christ's death of its meaning.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The Antioch scene describes a real failure and its correction. The doctrine in verses 15 to 21 is prescriptive truth for all time: justification is by faith alone, union with Christ is the believer's new reality, and grace must not be set aside.

debates.question: Does faith in Jesus Christ mean our faith in Him or the faithfulness of Christ?

views.label: Faith in Christ, the objective genitive
views.case: The historic and most natural reading takes it as the believer's faith directed to Christ; verse 16 adds so we too have believed in Christ Jesus, making our act of trust explicit.

views.label: The faithfulness of Christ, the subjective genitive
views.case: Some scholars render it as Christ's own faithfulness in His obedient death, emphasizing His work as the ground of justification.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that Christ's work is the sole basis of justification; the historic reading rightly keeps faith as the empty hand that receives Him, and the surrounding words make the believer's believing plain.

debates.question: What are works of the law that cannot justify?

views.label: All deeds done in obedience to the law
views.case: The Reformation reading takes works of the law broadly as any human law keeping offered as a ground of acceptance, since verse 16 says by works of the law no one will be justified.

views.label: Chiefly the boundary markers of Jewish identity
views.case: The new perspective stresses badges like circumcision, food laws, and days that marked Jews off from Gentiles, read from the Antioch table dispute.

debates.bearing: The context includes the identity badges, yet Paul's sweeping no one will be justified reaches every kind of law keeping; the historic reading is foregrounded, that no works of any sort can justify a sinner.

study.originalLanguages: The heartbeat of the passage is dikaioo, to justify, the judge's verdict that declares a sinner righteous. Paul states three times over that this verdict never comes by works of the nomos, the law, but only by pistis, faith, in Jesus Christ. The law can demand righteousness but cannot give it, so law keeping is the wrong road entirely. Then Paul reaches for systauroo, crucified together with Christ: in union with the Savior the old self under the law has died, and the risen Christ lives in the believer, who now lives by faith in the Son of God who loved him and gave Himself for him.

typology.type: Dying to the law to live to God
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the death and resurrection pattern; the old life under the law's condemnation ends and new life in Christ begins, foreshadowed by the exodus from bondage to service of God.
The believer's union with the crucified and risen Lord fulfills every sacrifice where a substitute died so the guilty could live.

study.christ: Christ dominates the passage. He is the object of justifying faith, the one in whom sinners are declared righteous. He is the one crucified, with whom the believer is crucified so that He now lives in them. He is the Son of God who loved me and gave Himself up for me, words that make the atonement gloriously personal. And He is the one whose death would be pointless if righteousness could be gained by law, so the sufficiency of His cross is the ground of the whole argument.
study.god: God is the righteous judge who justifies, pronouncing the guilty righteous for Christ's sake. He is the God of grace, and Paul refuses to set that grace aside. The passage exalts a God who does not lower His standard of righteousness but satisfies it in His Son, so that He can be both just and the justifier of those who have faith in Jesus. To add our works is to insult His grace and belittle His Son's death.

crossReferences.note: All are justified freely by grace through faith in Christ, apart from works of the law.
Believers united with Christ in His death and raised to walk in new life, echoing crucified with Christ.
Saved by grace through faith, not by works, so no one can boast.
A righteousness not from the law but through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God by faith.

study.oneStory: The Bible's great question is how sinful people can stand accepted before a holy God. From the sacrifices of the Old Testament to the cross, the answer has always pointed to a substitute. Here it is stated plainly: justification is by faith in Christ, who loved us and gave Himself for us. The believer, crucified with Christ, lives a wholly new life, and the grace of God stands unspoiled, for Christ did not die for nothing.

sermon.title: Justification by Faith Alone (Galatians 2:11 to 21)
sermon.why: Begg expounds Paul's confrontation with Peter and the great thesis that a person is justified by faith in Christ and not by works of the law, drawing out the meaning of being crucified with Christ and living by faith in the Son of God.

quiz.q: Why did Paul confront Peter at Antioch?

options.text: Because his withdrawal from Gentile believers denied the truth of the gospel.
options.note: Peter's separation implied Gentiles were second class without the law.

options.text: Because Peter had preached a false sermon.
options.note: The issue was Peter's conduct at the table, not a sermon.

options.text: Because of a personal grudge between them.
options.note: Paul acted to defend the gospel, not out of personal offense.

options.text: Because Peter had denied being an apostle.
options.note: Peter's apostleship was not in question; his hypocrisy was.

quiz.q: How is a person justified, according to verse 16?

options.text: By faith in Jesus Christ, not by works of the law.
options.note: The verse states the thesis three times over.

options.text: By a combination of faith and law keeping.
options.note: Paul excludes works of the law entirely as a ground of justification.

options.text: By sincere effort to obey the commandments.
options.note: By works of the law no one will be justified.

options.text: By being born into the right family.
options.note: Even Jews by birth are justified only by faith in Christ.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by I have been crucified with Christ?

options.text: In union with Christ his old self has died, and Christ now lives in him.
options.note: Verse 20 describes union with Christ in His death and life.

options.text: That he must earn salvation by suffering like Christ.
options.note: It is union with Christ's death, not a way to earn salvation.

options.text: That he no longer has any real personal existence.
options.note: He still lives in the body, but now by faith in the Son of God.

options.text: That the crucifixion was only symbolic.
options.note: The cross is utterly real; Paul is joined to the crucified Christ.

quiz.q: What would follow if righteousness could come through the law?

options.text: Then Christ died for nothing.
options.note: Verse 21 draws the stark conclusion of adding law to grace.

options.text: Then the law would need to be kept more strictly.
options.note: The point is not stricter law but that Christ's death would be pointless.

options.text: Then faith would still be the main thing.
options.note: If law could justify, faith in Christ would be unnecessary, not merely secondary.

options.text: Then God's grace would be even greater.
options.note: It would empty grace, not magnify it.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe Christ's love in verse 20?

options.text: The Son of God who loved me and gave Himself up for me.
options.note: The atonement is made deeply personal.

options.text: A distant love shown only to the whole world in general.
options.note: Paul makes it personal, loved me and gave Himself for me.

options.text: A love that depends on how well Paul performs.
options.note: The love is grounded in Christ's self giving, not Paul's performance.

options.text: A love shown only to the apostles.
options.note: It is the love every believer receives in Christ.

questions: Peter's fear of others led him to act against the gospel he believed. Where does fear of what people think tempt you to live inconsistently with the gospel?
Justification means God declares you righteous by faith in Christ, not by your performance. How would resting in that verdict change the way you face your failures?
Paul says Christ lives in me and I live by faith in the Son of God. What does it look like, practically, to live each day by faith in Christ rather than by self effort?
To add works to grace is to say Christ died for nothing. What are you tempted to add to Christ as if His finished work were not enough?
Christ loved me and gave Himself up for me. How does hearing the atonement in the first person, as personal love for you, move your heart today?

translationNotes.note: Faith in Jesus Christ renders a Greek phrase some translate as the faithfulness of Christ; the added clause so we too have believed in Christ Jesus supports the historic reading that it is the believer's faith directed to Christ.

The BSB footnote observes that some translators close Paul's quotation to Peter after verse 16 or 21; the doctrinal statement flows from the confrontation either way, and the point to Peter is the point to Galatia.
Crucified with Christ renders systauroo, crucified together with; it describes the believer's union with Christ in His death, not a call to earn salvation by suffering.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 2:11-21. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg.
